sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Eight — graphing linear equations. Today we turn equations into pictures. Once you can see a line, the equation behind it stops feeling abstract and starts feeling obvious.

Quick scene. You open Uber. The fare is two dollars and fifty cents to start, plus a dollar fifty per mile. That's an equation: y equals one point five x plus two point five. But here's the thing — we can draw that. And once we draw it, you can look at the picture and instantly tell what a ten-mile ride costs, without doing the arithmetic. Seeing the line is faster than reading the numbers.

Three goals today. One — understand the slope-intercept form, y equals m x plus b. Two — plot any line straight from its equation. Three — read a graph backwards into an equation. By the end, equations and lines will feel like two views of the same thing.

Quick refresher on the playing field. The Cartesian plane — two axes, x going across, y going up. They cross at the origin, point zero comma zero. That splits the plane into four quadrants, numbered counter-clockwise starting top right. Every point gets an x-coordinate and a y-coordinate. That's it — that's our canvas.

Meet the slope-intercept form. y equals m x plus b. The m is the slope — how steep the line is, rise over run. The b is the y-intercept — where the line crosses the y-axis. So if I write y equals two x plus three, the slope is two and the y-intercept is three. Two numbers, one line, no mystery.

Let's plot y equals two x plus three. Step one — mark the y-intercept at the point zero comma three. Step two — slope is two, which means rise two, run one. From zero comma three, go up two, right one. That gives you the point one comma five. Step three — connect those two dots with a straight line and extend it both ways. Done. That's the graph.

When slope-intercept feels shaky, fall back to a table. Take y equals negative x plus four. Pick a few x values. When x is zero, y is four. When x is one, y is three. When x is two, y is two. Plot those three points. They line up perfectly. Connect them, extend the line. Same line — just built point by point.

Sometimes the equation isn't in slope-intercept form. Take two x plus three y equals twelve. Use intercepts. Set y to zero — two x equals twelve — x equals six. So six comma zero is the x-intercept. Set x to zero — three y equals twelve — y equals four. So zero comma four is the y-intercept. Plot both intercepts, connect them, line drawn.
Your turn. Plot the line y equals negative x plus four. Y-intercept is four. Slope is negative one — down one, right one. Pause the video, sketch the line, come back when you've got it.

Time. Y-intercept at zero comma four. From there, slope of negative one — down one, right one — takes you to one comma three. Draw a straight line through those two points and extend it. The line falls gently as you read left to right. If your line went up, you flipped the sign — easy fix, just remember negative slope means falling.

Two quick rules about lines and slope. Parallel lines never meet — that happens exactly when they share the same slope. Perpendicular lines cross at a perfect right angle — that happens when their slopes are negative reciprocals. So a line with slope two is perpendicular to a line with slope negative one half. Multiply them — you get negative one. Every time.

Back to Uber. y equals one point five x plus two point five. Graph it. The line crosses the y-axis at two point five — that's your base fee, charged before the car even moves. The slope is one point five — that's the cost per mile. The graph is now a price chart. A ten-mile ride? Read off the line — about seventeen dollars fifty. No calculator needed.

One common mistake — flipping x and y when you plot. The point three comma two means x is three and y is two. Go right three, then up two. Not the other way around. X always first, then y. Horizontal, then vertical. Get this reflex right and ninety percent of plotting errors disappear.

Recap. The slope-intercept form, y equals m x plus b — m is slope, b is the y-intercept. Three ways to plot — from slope-intercept, from intercepts, or from a table of points. Same slope means parallel. Negative reciprocal slopes means perpendicular. Your assignment is in the dashboard — graph four lines from equations, then write equations from two graphs.

Here's how to actually master this. The video is the easy ten percent. Read OpenStax chapter three point two on graphing linear equations. Then twenty problems on Khan Academy — graphing slope-intercept form. Hand in the dashboard assignment. If anything still feels foggy, book your advisor. Next up — Module Nine, where we put a number on how steep any line actually is.
